learning_outcomes.description: Demonstrate a familiarity with the key theories and debates that have emerged within the fields of urban sociology;

Identify the connections between local (urban) and global processes. This includes understanding the dimensions of lived space and the impacts that processes such as industrialisation, globalisation and postmodernity have had on them;
Demonstrate knowledge of the historical development of Australian cities, suburbs and identify how the Australian experience of urban life differs from and resembles other countries;

Demonstrate sound analytical skills, and a developed 'sociological imagination', by identifying how peoples experiences of urban life are both influenced by, and also affect, factors such as social class, race, age, gender and consumption;
Communicate sociological understandings and analyses of the city to both professional/academic and lay audiences in a variety of media.

assessments.task_description:
Due Date: To be submitted at the end of class in Weeks 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 11 and 12

Weighting: 50%

This assessment is designed to help you practise critical thinking skills by applying them to the content and concepts learned during the course. 

During seminars in Weeks 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 11 and 12, you will complete a written response to a problem question/s set by the lecturer during the final 30 minutes of class. Your response will require you to apply what you have learnt from the readings and seminar content for each topic since the last assessment, addressing 'real-world' urban problems or phenomena. 

Assessment Security Requirements:   

These are secure, identity-verified assessments with the following requirements:

  • This assessment can only be completed in-person during the seminar
  • No digital devices are permitted - Computers, phones, tablets, or other digital devices cannot be used and must be put away during the activity.    
  • Paper-based responses only - You must complete responses on the paper writing materials provided by the lecturer.  
  • You may use one A4 page of handwritten notes to assist your memory during the activity.

Grading Structure:

Seven of these assessments will be conducted over the course of the semester, each worth 10 marks. You may attempt all of them; however, only your best 5 scores will count toward your final grade, for a maximum of 50 marks.


Marking criteria: An assessment rubric is available in the ‘Assessments’ folder on the course Blackboard site. 

AI Use: This assessment task will be completed in-person. The use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT) tools will not be permitted. Any attempted use of AI or MT may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Assessment completed in-class and collected by the course coordinator or facilitator when complete.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: You will receive a mark of 0 if this assessment is submitted late.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

assessments.task_description:
This assessment is worth 50 percent of your total semester marks.

Because this course is about space and place and how social relations are made possible in space and place, this assessment asks you use both video and commentary to create a 7-10 minute critical mini-documentary about an urban place of your choosing. 

The purpose of the assessment is for you to apply what you have learnt during the semester to a place in the city you chose (not a whole city), effectively telling a story about that place but using the sociological concepts we discussed during the semester. We will have plenty of time to think and discuss this assessment and choose your place during the semester, and more details will be available on Blackboard. You will receive training in how to script and storyboard your video, as well as how to shoot effective video with your smartphone and basic video editing using free software. You can cooperate with other students, friends and family for production, props, interviewees etc, but the final product, including all video footage and voice commentary, must be your own work.

Note: For identity verification purposes, you need to speak to the camera for at least 30 seconds in each: (1) during the introduction (2) in the location at your selected place and (3) during the conclusion of your documentary.

For this assessment, you need only a smartphone to record video and audio. Place selection, creativity, and conceptual content will be assessed rather than technical video production proficiency. 

Videos must be uploaded by the due date. Instructions on how to do this will be available on Blackboard.

Marking criteria and/or marking rubrics are available in the ‘Assessment’ folder in Blackboard for this course.

AI Use: This task has been designed to be challenging, authentic and complex. Whilst students may use AI technologies, successful completion of assessment in this course will require students to critically engage in specific contexts and tasks for which AI may provide limited support and guidance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct Policy. Students may be required to complete an AI usage declaration and submit it with their assessment. Additional resources are available through UQ AI Student Hub.
assessments.submission_guidelines: This assessment item must be submitted via Turnitin on Blackboard Ultra.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction: The city and us

The purpose of this first week is to provide students with an overview of the course content, assessment and objectives. An introduction to the major themes will be provided as well as a discussion outlining what we understand a city to be.


Learning outcomes: L01, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction: The city and us The purpose of this first week is to provide students with an overview of the course content, assessment and objectives. An introduction to the major themes will be provided as well as a discussion outlining  what we understand a city to be.

learning_activities.topic:
Space and place

We will explore some of the basic concepts that inform urban sociology, that is, how a space can be made social, how spaces become places, and the importance of who or what creates those places. We also explore the ways that humans live in homes and the importance attached to them; and finally the concept of community, its myriad meanings and how and why it is invoked so often in contemporary Western society. These are all concepts that will be re-visited throughout the coming weeks and the purpose is to provide you with a good foundation for the remainder of your study this semester.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Space and place We will explore some of the basic concepts that inform urban sociology, that is, how a space can be made social, how spaces become places, and the importance of who or what creates those places. We also explore the ways that humans live in homes and the importance attached to them; and finally the concept of community, its myriad meanings and how and why it is invoked so often in contemporary Western society. These are all concepts that will be re-visited throughout the coming weeks and the purpose is to provide you with a good foundation for the remainder of your study this semester.

learning_activities.topic:
Home and community

We will explore some of the basic sociological concepts that inform urban sociology, that is, the ways that humans live in homes and the importance attached to them, and the concept of community, its myriad meanings and how and why it is invoked so often in contemporary Western society. These are all concepts that will be revisited throughout the coming weeks, and the purpose is to provide you with a good foundation for the remainder of your study this semester.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Home and community We will explore some of the basic sociological concepts that inform urban sociology, that is,  the ways that humans live in homes and the importance attached to them, and the concept of community, its myriad meanings and how and why it is invoked so often in contemporary Western society. These are all concepts that will be revisited throughout the coming weeks, and the purpose is to provide you with a good foundation for the remainder of your study this semester.

learning_activities.topic:
The Modern to the Postmodern City

This week, we look at the social history of the city. We will focus mainly on the city as we recognise it today, the form that emerged after the industrial revolution, but we will also look further back into history to trace the early roots and rationale for the existence of cities. We will expand on last week's discussion of space, place, home and community, particularly the radical changes that occurred to the ways in which social lives and relationships changed after the industrial revolution and into postmodernity.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: The Modern to the Postmodern City This week, we look at the social history of the city. We will focus mainly on the city as we recognise it today, the form that emerged after the industrial revolution, but we will also look further back into history to trace the early roots and rationale for the existence of cities. We will expand on last week's discussion of space, place, home and community, particularly the radical changes that occurred to the ways in which social lives and relationships changed after the industrial revolution and into postmodernity.

learning_activities.topic:
Gentrification and change

Urban community, gentrification and change (Lecture): Urban community, gentrification and change: The Australian government at every level has made a commitment to the ‘compact city’, where existing infrastructure and urban space is used to increase population density rather than adding to the existing urban sprawl. There are social consequences to this policy and this week we will use the case of West End to explore the nature of an inner-city urban community, issues of lifestyle, gentrification and how, and why, residents resist and adapt to change.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Gentrification and change Urban community, gentrification and change (Lecture): Urban community, gentrification and change: The Australian government at every level has made a commitment to the ‘compact city’, where existing infrastructure and urban space is used to increase population density rather than adding to the existing urban sprawl. There are social consequences to this policy and this week we will use the case of West End to explore the nature of an inner-city urban community, issues of lifestyle, gentrification and how, and why, residents resist and adapt to change.

learning_activities.topic:
A suburban world and The Australian Dream

What is suburb? Why are they important? This century, according to many commentators is the Century of the suburb and suburban life is how most urban dwellers will experience the city in the 21st Century. This week we examine why this is important, then focus on the unique place that the suburb has in Australian life.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_title: A suburban world and The Australian Dream
learning_activities.topic_description: A suburban world and The Australian Dream What is suburb? Why are they important? This century, according to many commentators is the Century of the suburb and suburban life is how most urban dwellers will experience the city in the 21st Century. This week we examine why this is important, then focus on the unique place that the suburb has in Australian life.

learning_activities.topic:
Privatopias: The disappearing public realm

This week we take a closer look at the concept of the ‘public realm’ and the tensions that arise between access for all to the space in a city and private property rights. We examine some recent and not so recent phenomena in urban development - the ‘gated community’ and the ‘master planned estate’, as well as the shopping mall; all created by capital for the more or less exclusive use of residents. We will use examples from both Australia and the US to show how these places appeal to the postmodern concept of the biographical construction of their target markets and discuss what sort of contribution is made to the ‘commons’ by these places.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05

We will use examples from both Australia and the US to show how these places appeal to the postmodern concept of the biographical construction of their target markets and discuss what sort of contribution is made to the ‘commons’ by these places.

learning_activities.topic:
Documentary week

Now that you have completed your script ands storyboarding for you mini documentary, you can use this week to make a start on your site work and become familiar with the editing process.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Documentary week Now that you have completed your script ands storyboarding for you mini documentary, you can use this week to make a start on your site work and become familiar with the editing process.

learning_activities.topic:
Marginalisation and the indigenous city

We focus on marginalised groups in the city with a special focus on the perspectives of Indigenous people, both in Australia and overseas to try and understand more about how first nations people see urban places shaped by others. 


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Marginalisation and the indigenous city We focus on marginalised groups in the city with a special focus on the perspectives of Indigenous people, both in Australia and overseas to try and understand more about how first nations people see urban places shaped by others.

learning_activities.topic:
Informality and and the Southern City

This week we turn our attention to how the majority of the worlds urban population live - in the cities of the global south. We focus on the 'informal city', slums and slum life and the idea of the 'urban subaltern'.


Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Informality and and the Southern City This week we turn our attention to how the majority of the worlds urban population live - in the cities of the global south. We focus on the 'informal city', slums and slum life and the idea of the 'urban subaltern'.

learning_activities.topic:
The City and the Environment

The city is seen as both the cause and possibly the saviour for current environmental challenges facing our planet. This week we look at the ways in which cities and various forms of living might contribute to environmental problems, the ways that planning and development might address these problems and the associated social conditions and consequences that we must consider. We will use examples from around the world and discuss their relevance to Australian urban conditions and practises.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
